name: Nazca Astronaut Figure
description: The 'Nazca Astronaut Figure' refers to one of the many geoglyphs found in the Nazca Desert of southern Peru. Proponents of alternative theories suggest this particular figure, officially designated as 'The Giant' or 'El Gigante', resembles an astronaut or extraterrestrial being. This interpretation gained popularity through figures like Erich von Däniken, who, in his book 'Chariots of the Gods?', proposed that the Nazca Lines were created by ancient astronauts who used them as landing strips or signals. The figure's large size, depiction of a head with large eyes, and what some interpret as a 'helmet' or 'hood' fuel this speculation. The theory posits that ancient civilizations were visited and influenced by extraterrestrial beings, who imparted advanced knowledge and technologies. Mainstream archaeology rejects this interpretation, viewing the figure as a stylized representation of a human-like deity or figure within the context of Nazca culture and religious beliefs. Archaeological evidence suggests that the Nazca Lines, including 'The Giant', were created by the Nazca people between 500 BCE and 500 CE using simple tools and surveying techniques. The lines are believed to have served ritualistic or ceremonial purposes, possibly related to agriculture, water management, or astronomical observations. The 'astronaut' interpretation is considered pseudoscientific due to a lack of credible evidence and reliance on selective interpretation of the figure's features and the broader archaeological context. The lack of any actual alien artifacts or technology at the site further undermines the claim. The theory persists due to its appeal to a desire for extraordinary explanations for ancient mysteries.
subtitle: Ancient astronaut or misinterpreted deity?

claims.claim: The figure resembles an astronaut in a spacesuit.
claims.evidence: The figure's head is large and rounded, and some interpret a marking around the head as a helmet.
claims.criticism: The figure's features are stylized and open to interpretation. There is no concrete evidence to suggest it represents an astronaut, and the 'helmet' interpretation is subjective.

claims.claim: The Nazca Lines were created to guide or signal extraterrestrial spacecraft.
claims.evidence: The lines are long and straight, resembling landing strips.
claims.criticism: The lines vary in length and direction, and many are not straight. There is no evidence of any aircraft landing or taking off from the lines. The lines served ceremonial purposes.

claims.claim: Ancient astronauts visited and influenced the Nazca civilization.
claims.evidence: The Nazca Lines demonstrate advanced knowledge or technology that the Nazca people could not have possessed.
claims.criticism: The Nazca people were capable of creating the lines using simple tools and surveying techniques. There is no evidence of extraterrestrial influence or technology.

keyFigures.name: Erich von Däniken
keyFigures.role: Popularized the ancient astronaut theory and applied it to the Nazca Lines.

keyFigures.name: Anthony Aveni
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who studies archaeoastronomy and the Nazca Lines, providing mainstream interpretations.

timeline.event: Creation of the Nazca Lines by the Nazca culture
Erich von Däniken publishes 'Chariots of the Gods?', popularizing the ancient astronaut theory.
The 'Nazca Astronaut Figure' continues to be cited as evidence by proponents of ancient astronaut theories.

sources.author: Anthony F. Aveni
sources.work: Between the Lines: The Mystery of the Giant Ground Drawings of Ancient Nasca, Peru
sources.title: Anthony F. Aveni

sources.author: Johan Reinhard
sources.work: The Nazca Lines: A New Perspective on their Origin and Meaning
sources.title: Johan Reinhard
